name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلْنَـٰهُ لِلنَّاسِ سَوَآءً ٱلْعَـٰكِفُ فِيهِ وَٱلْبَادِ ۚ وَمَن يُرِدْ فِيهِ بِإِلْحَادٍۭ بِظُلْمٍ نُّذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٢٥)
```

```english
Indeed those who are faithless and bar from the way of Allah and the Sacred Mosque, which We have made for all people, the native and the visitor being equal therein—whoever wrongfully tries to commit violation in it, We shall make him taste a painful punishment. (25)
```

```arabic
وَإِذْ بَوَّأْنَا لِإِبْرَٰهِيمَ مَكَانَ ٱلْبَيْتِ أَن لَّا تُشْرِكْ بِى شَيْـًٔا وَطَهِّرْ بَيْتِىَ لِلطَّآئِفِينَ وَٱلْقَآئِمِينَ وَٱلرُّكَّعِ ٱلسُّجُودِ (٢٦)
```

```english
When We settled for Abraham the site of the House [saying], Do not ascribe any partners to Me, and purify My House for those who circle around it, and those who stand [in it for prayer], and those who bow and prostrate themselves. (26)
```

```arabic
وَأَذِّن فِى ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْحَجِّ يَأْتُوكَ رِجَالًا وَعَلَىٰ كُلِّ ضَامِرٍ يَأْتِينَ مِن كُلِّ فَجٍّ عَمِيقٍ (٢٧)
```

```english
And proclaim the Hajj to all the people: they will come to you on foot and on lean camels, coming from distant places, (27)
```

```arabic
لِّيَشْهَدُوا۟ مَنَـٰفِعَ لَهُمْ وَيَذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ فِىٓ أَيَّامٍ مَّعْلُومَـٰتٍ عَلَىٰ مَا رَزَقَهُم مِّنۢ بَهِيمَةِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ ۖ فَكُلُوا۟ مِنْهَا وَأَطْعِمُوا۟ ٱلْبَآئِسَ ٱلْفَقِيرَ (٢٨)
```

```english
that they may witness the benefits for them, and mention Allah’s Name during the known days over the livestock He has provided them. So eat thereof and feed the destitute and the needy. (28)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ لْيَقْضُوا۟ تَفَثَهُمْ وَلْيُوفُوا۟ نُذُورَهُمْ وَلْيَطَّوَّفُوا۟ بِٱلْبَيْتِ ٱلْعَتِيقِ (٢٩)
```

```english
Then let them do away with their untidiness, fulfill their vows, and circle around the Ancient House. (29)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ وَمَن يُعَظِّمْ حُرُمَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُۥ عِندَ رَبِّهِۦ ۗ وَأُحِلَّتْ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمُ إِلَّا مَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ ٱلرِّجْسَ مِنَ ٱلْأَوْثَـٰنِ وَٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ قَوْلَ ٱلزُّورِ (٣٠)
```

```english
That, and whoever venerates the sacraments of Allah, that is better for him with his Lord. You are permitted [animals of] grazing livestock except for what will be recited to you. So avoid the abomination of idols, and avoid false speech, (30)
```

```arabic
حُنَفَآءَ لِلَّهِ غَيْرَ مُشْرِكِينَ بِهِۦ ۚ وَمَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَكَأَنَّمَا خَرَّ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَتَخْطَفُهُ ٱلطَّيْرُ أَوْ تَهْوِى بِهِ ٱلرِّيحُ فِى مَكَانٍ سَحِيقٍ (٣١)
```

```english
as persons having pure faith in Allah, not ascribing partners to Him. Whoever ascribes partners to Allah is as though he had fallen from a height, then [his corpse] is devoured by vultures, or [his remains are] blown away by the wind far and wide. (31)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ وَمَن يُعَظِّمْ شَعَـٰٓئِرَ ٱللَّهِ فَإِنَّهَا مِن تَقْوَى ٱلْقُلُوبِ (٣٢)
```

```english
That. And whoever venerates the sacraments of Allah—indeed that arises from the Godwariness of hearts. (32)
```

```arabic
لَكُمْ فِيهَا مَنَـٰفِعُ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ثُمَّ مَحِلُّهَآ إِلَى ٱلْبَيْتِ ٱلْعَتِيقِ (٣٣)
```

```english
You may benefit from them until a specified time. Then their place of sacrifice is by the Ancient House. (33)
```

```arabic
وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ جَعَلْنَا مَنسَكًا لِّيَذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَىٰ مَا رَزَقَهُم مِّنۢ بَهِيمَةِ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ ۗ فَإِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ فَلَهُۥٓ أَسْلِمُوا۟ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُخْبِتِينَ (٣٤)
```

```english
For every nation We have appointed a rite so that they might mention Allah’s Name over the livestock He has provided them. Your God is the One God, so submit to Him. And give good news to the humble (34)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ذُكِرَ ٱللَّهُ وَجِلَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَصَابَهُمْ وَٱلْمُقِيمِى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَمِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ يُنفِقُونَ (٣٥)
```

```english
—those whose hearts tremble with awe when Allah is mentioned, and who are patient through whatever visits them, and who maintain the prayer and spend out of what We have provided them. (35)
```

```arabic
وَٱلْبُدْنَ جَعَلْنَـٰهَا لَكُم مِّن شَعَـٰٓئِرِ ٱللَّهِ لَكُمْ فِيهَا خَيْرٌ ۖ فَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهَا صَوَآفَّ ۖ فَإِذَا وَجَبَتْ جُنُوبُهَا فَكُلُوا۟ مِنْهَا وَأَطْعِمُوا۟ ٱلْقَانِعَ وَٱلْمُعْتَرَّ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ سَخَّرْنَـٰهَا لَكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٣٦)
```

```english
We have appointed for you the [sacrificial] camels as part of Allah’s sacraments. There is good for you in them. So mention the Name of Allah over them as they stand. And when they have fallen on their flanks, eat from them, and feed the self-contained needy and the mendicant. Thus have We disposed them for your benefit so that you may give thanks. (36)
```

```arabic
لَن يَنَالَ ٱللَّهَ لُحُومُهَا وَلَا دِمَآؤُهَا وَلَـٰكِن يَنَالُهُ ٱلتَّقْوَىٰ مِنكُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ سَخَّرَهَا لَكُمْ لِتُكَبِّرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ مَا هَدَىٰكُمْ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٣٧)
```

```english
It is not their flesh or blood that reaches Allah; rather, it is your piety that reaches Him. Thus has He disposed them for your benefit so that you may magnify Allah for His guiding you. And give good news to the virtuous. (37)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses mention the polytheists' (المشركين) barring of the believers from the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام), rebuking them with threats. They also refer to the initial legislation of the Pilgrimage (الحج) to the House for Ibrahim (peace be upon him), His command to him to proclaim the Pilgrimage among the people, and a number of the rulings of the Pilgrimage.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَيَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَالْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ الَّذِي جَعَلْناهُ لِلنَّاسِ*» — **Indeed those who are faithless and keep [others] from the way of Allah and the Sacred Mosque, which We have appointed for all the people** (22:25) etc. **Barring** (الصد) means preventing. **Equally** (سَواءً) is a verbal noun (مصدر) used in the sense of an active participle. **Staying** (العكوف) in a place means residing in it. **The visitor** (البادي) is derived from *badw* (البدو), which means appearing; it refers—as has been said—to the newcomer, meaning the one who intends to reach it from outside and enters it. **Deviation** (الإلحاد) means inclining away from rectitude; its linguistic origin refers to the slanting of an animal's hoof.

What is meant by **those who are faithless** (الذين كفروا) are the polytheists of Mecca who disbelieved in the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) at the beginning of the mission before the Migration (الهجرة). They used to prevent people from Islam—which is the way of Allah (سبيل الله)—and prevent the believers from entering the Sacred Mosque to perform the circumambulation (الطواف) of the Kaaba, establish prayer, and perform the rest of the rites (المناسك). Thus, His saying: **and keep [others]** (يَصُدُّونَ) denotes continuity (الاستمرار). There is no harm in its being conjoined to the past tense verb in His saying **those who are faithless** (الذين كفروا). The meaning is: those who disbelieved previously and continue to prevent people from the way of Allah and the believers from the Sacred Mosque.

By this, it becomes clear that His saying: **and the Sacred Mosque** (وَالْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرامِ) is conjoined to **the way of Allah** (سَبِيلِ اللهِ). What is meant by their barring it is their preventing the believers from performing acts of worship and rites therein. A necessary consequence of this was preventing those intending the House from outside Mecca from entering it.

Through this, it is clarified that the meaning of His saying: **which We have appointed for all the people** (الَّذِي جَعَلْناهُ لِلنَّاسِ)—which is a description of the Sacred Mosque—is that He appointed it for the worship of the people, not that He gave them legal ownership of its physical structure. Thus, the people possess the right to worship Allah therein, and no one has the right to prevent anyone from that. In this is an indication that their prevention and barring from the Sacred Mosque is a transgression on their part against the right of the people and a deviation (إلحاد) through wrongdoing (بظلم), just as the annexation of "the way" to "Allah" indicates their transgression against the right of Allah the Exalted.

This is also supported by His following it with His saying: **the seeker of retirement therein and the visitor being equal** (سَواءً الْعاكِفُ فِيهِ وَالْبادِ). That is, the resident therein and the one coming from outside are equal in that both have the right of worshiping Allah therein. What is meant by "residing therein" and "being outside of it" is either residing in Mecca and being outside of it—by way of intellectual metaphor (المجاز العقلي)—or the constant attendance of the Mosque for worship versus arriving at it for that purpose.

His saying: **and whoever desires to deviate therein wrongfully, We shall make him taste a painful punishment** (وَمَنْ يُرِدْ فِيهِ بِإِلْحادٍ بِظُلْمٍ نُذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ) is an explanation of the recompense for whoever wrongs the people regarding this right legislated for them in the Mosque. Its necessary implication is the prohibition of barring people from entering it to worship therein. The object of **desires** (يُرِدْ) is omitted to indicate generality. The preposition *bi* in **to deviate** (بِإِلْحادٍ) denotes accompaniment (الملابسة), and in **wrongfully** (بِظُلْمٍ) it denotes causality (السببية). The sentence serves as the predicate (خبر) for His saying: **Indeed those who are faithless** (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا) at the beginning of the verse.

The meaning is: those who disbelieved and continue to prevent people from the way of Allah—which is the religion of Islam—and prevent the believers from the Sacred Mosque, which We have made a place of worship for the people where the resident and the visitor are equal, We shall make them taste a painful punishment because they "desire" the people therein with deviation through wrongdoing; and whoever desires the people therein with deviation through wrongdoing, We shall make him taste a painful punishment.

The exegetes have many varied opinions regarding the grammatical analysis of the individual words and sentences of the verse, but perhaps what we have presented is most suitable to the context (السياق).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَإِذْ بَوَّأْنا لِإِبْراهِيمَ مَكانَ الْبَيْتِ أَنْ لا تُشْرِكْ بِي شَيْئاً وَطَهِّرْ بَيْتِيَ لِلطَّائِفِينَ وَالْقائِمِينَ وَالرُّكَّعِ السُّجُودِ*» — **When We settled for Ibrahim the site of the House: ‘Do not ascribe any partners to Me, and purify My House for those who go around it, and those who stand [in it for prayer], and those who bow and prostrate.’** (22:26). To **settle** (بوء) a place for someone means to make it a dwelling (مباءة) and a place of return (مرجع) for him, which he returns to and intends. **The site** (المكان) is that upon which a thing rests; thus, the site of the House is the piece of land upon which it was built. What is meant by **those who stand** (القائمين), according to what the context provides, are those who devote themselves to worship and prayer. **The bowing** (الركع) is the plural of *rāki‘* (راكع), just as *sujjad* (سجد) is the plural of *sājid* (ساجد), and **the prostrating** (السجود) is the plural of *sājid* (ساجد), like *rukū‘* (الركوع) is the plural of *rāki‘* (راكع).

His saying: **When We settled for Ibrahim the site of the House** (وَإِذْ بَوَّأْنا لِإِبْراهِيمَ مَكانَ الْبَيْتِ)—the adverbial phrase (الظرف) therein is connected to an omitted [verb], meaning: "And remember the time when..." In this is a reminder of the story of making the House a place of worship for the people, so that it becomes clear that barring the believers from the Sacred Mosque is nothing but deviation through wrongdoing.

His settling the site of the House for Ibrahim was making its site a place of return and a reference point for His worship, not for him to take it as a residential house to live in. This is hinted at by His saying afterward: **purify My House** (طَهِّرْ بَيْتِيَ), by annexing the House to Himself. There is no doubt that this "settling" was a revelation (وحي) to Ibrahim. Thus, His saying: **We settled for Ibrahim the site of the House** (بَوَّأْنا لِإِبْراهِيمَ مَكانَ الْبَيْتِ) is in the sense of our saying: "We revealed to Ibrahim to take this place as a dwelling and a place of return for My worship," or if you wish: "We revealed to him to intend this place for My worship," or in other words: "Worship Me in this place."

By this, it becomes clear that the particle *an* (أَنْ) in His saying: **‘Do not ascribe any partners to Me’** (أَنْ لا تُشْرِكْ بِي شَيْئاً) is explanatory (مفسرة), explaining the preceding revelation insofar as it is a "saying," without the need to estimate [the words] "We revealed" or "We said" and the like.

It also becomes clear that His saying: **‘Do not ascribe any partners to Me’** (أَنْ لا تُشْرِكْ بِي شَيْئاً)—occurring in this context—does not mean the prohibition of polytheism (الشرك) in an absolute sense, even though it is prohibited absolutely. Rather, what is prohibited here is polytheism in the worship that he performs when he intends the House for worship. In clearer terms: polytheism in the acts of Pilgrimage he performs, such as the ritual call (التلبية) to idols, the consecration (الإهلال) to them, and the like.

Likewise, His saying: **‘and purify My House for those who go around it, and those who stand [in it for prayer], and those who bow and prostrate.’** (وَطَهِّرْ بَيْتِيَ لِلطَّائِفِينَ وَالْقائِمِينَ وَالرُّكَّعِ السُّجُودِ). **Purification** (التطهير) is the removal of filth and impurities from a thing so that it returns to what its primary nature (طبعه الأولي) requires. He has annexed the House to Himself when He said: **My House** (بَيْتِيَ), meaning a house dedicated to My worship. The purification of a place of worship, insofar as it is a place of worship, is its sanctification from filthy acts and **abominations** (الأرجاس) that corrupt worship, which are nothing other than polytheism and its manifestations.

Thus, the purification of His House is either its sanctification from spiritual abominations specifically—by Ibrahim (peace be upon him) legislating for the people and teaching them a way of worship that is not entered by the filth of polytheism nor defiled by its impurity, just as he was commanded for himself—or it is the removal of all impurities from the House, whether physical or spiritual. However, that which touches the context of the verse is the spiritual abomination. The sum of purifying the place of worship from spiritual abominations and sanctifying it for the worshipers who intend it for worship is the establishment of worship therein purely for the sake of Allah, untainted by any trace of polytheism, whereby they worship Allah, the Glorified, and do not associate anything with Him.

The meaning, based on what the context guides to, is: "And remember when We revealed to Ibrahim to worship Me in this House of Mine by taking it as a dwelling and a place of return for My worship; and do not associate anything with Me in My worship; and legislate for My servants who intend My House—from those who circumambulate, those who stand, and those who bow and prostrate—a worship in My House that is pure from polytheism."

In the verse, there is a hint that the primary worship of those intending it consists of circumambulation, standing, bowing, and prostration. There is also an indication that bowing and prostration are close to each other, as if they are inseparable, one not being detached from the other.

Among what has been said regarding the verse is that His saying: **We settled** (بَوَّأْنا) means "We said: Settle," and it was said it means "We informed." From this, [it is said] that *an* (أَنْ) in His saying **‘Do not...’** (أَنْ لا) is a subordinating conjunction (مصدرية), and it was said it is the lightened form of the heavy *anna* (مخففة من الثقيلة). Also, it was said that what is meant by **those who go around** (الطائفين) are the newcomers, and by **those who stand** (القائمين) are the residents of Mecca. It was also said that what is meant by **those who stand, and those who bow and prostrate** are those who pray. All of these are far-fetched views.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَأَذِّنْ فِي النَّاسِ بِالْحَجِّ يَأْتُوكَ رِجالاً وَعَلى كُلِّ ضامِرٍ يَأْتِينَ مِنْ كُلِّ فَجٍّ عَمِيقٍ*» — **And proclaim the Hajj to the people: they shall come to you on foot and on every lean camel coming from every distant pathway** (22:27). **Proclaiming** (التأذين) is informing by raising the voice, which is why it is interpreted as "calling out." **The Pilgrimage** (الحج) means "the intention" (القصد); the specific act first legislated by Ibrahim (peace be upon him) and continued in the law of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was named thus because it involves intending the Sacred House. **On foot** (رجالاً) is the plural of *rājil* (راجل), the opposite of a rider. **Lean** (الضامِر) refers to the emaciated [camel] that travel has made thin. **A distant pathway** (الفج العميق)—according to what has been said—is a far-off road.

His saying: **And proclaim the Hajj to the people** (وَأَذِّنْ فِي النَّاسِ بِالْحَجِّ) means: call the people to intend the House or to the act of Pilgrimage. The sentence is conjoined to His saying: **‘Do not ascribe any partners to Me’** (لا تُشْرِكْ بِي شَيْئاً), and the one addressed is Ibrahim. What has been said—that the one addressed is our Prophet Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—is far from the context.

His saying: **they shall come to you on foot** (يَأْتُوكَ رِجالاً) etc., is the response to the command. That is: proclaim among them, and if you proclaim among them, they shall come to you walking and on every emaciated camel coming from every far-off road. The word **every** (كُلِّ) in such instances conveys the meaning of "many" rather than "all-encompassing."

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*لِيَشْهَدُوا مَنافِعَ لَهُمْ وَيَذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللهِ فِي أَيَّامٍ مَعْلُوماتٍ*» — **that they may witness the benefits for them, and celebrate the name of Allah during the known days** (22:28) etc. The *lām* is for causality (التعليل) or purpose (الغاية). The prepositional phrase is connected to His saying: **they shall come to you** (يَأْتُوكَ). The meaning is: they shall come to you to witness benefits for them, or they shall come to you and thus witness benefits for them. The "benefits" have been mentioned generally, not restricted to the worldly or the otherworldly.

Benefits are of two types: Worldly benefits, which are those by which human social life advances, living becomes refined, various needs are met, and different deficiencies are completed—such as types of trade, politics, governance, administration, various protocols, etiquettes, traditions, customs, and different forms of social cooperation and mutual support, among others.

When peoples and nations from different regions and corners of the earth gather—despite their differences in lineage, color, traditions, and etiquettes—and their word is one, which is the word of Truth, and their God is one, who is Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه), and their direction is one, which is the Kaaba, the Sacred House; then the unity of spirits leads to the proximity of bodies, and the unity of word leads to the similarity of action. Thus, this one takes from that one what he approves of and gives him what pleases him. One people seeks help from others in solving their problems, and they help them with what is in their capacity. Thus, every partial society is replaced by a higher society. Then the societies blend and form a vast society possessing such strength and preparation that the firm mountains cannot stand against it, nor can any crushing tyrannical power overcome it. There is no means to solve the problems of life like mutual support, and no way to mutual support like mutual understanding, and no mutual understanding like the understanding of religion (الدين).

And otherworldly benefits, which are the ways of seeking proximity to Allah the Exalted through that which represents human servitude (العبادة) in word and deed. The act of Pilgrimage, with its rites, includes various types of worship: turning toward Allah, abandoning the pleasures of life and the preoccupations of living, striving toward Him by enduring hardships, circumambulating His House, prayer, sacrifice, spending, fasting, and other things.

It has been mentioned previously that the act of Pilgrimage, with its pillars and parts, represents a complete cycle of what occurred to Ibrahim (peace be upon him) in his journey through the stages of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* —, the negation of partners, and the sincerity of servitude to Allah, the Glorified.

Thus, the people's coming to Ibrahim (peace be upon him)—meaning their presence at the House to visit it—results in their witnessing these benefits, both otherworldly and worldly. When they witness them, they become attached to them, for man is instinctively disposed toward the love of benefit.

His saying: **and celebrate the name of Allah during the known days over the livestock He has provided them** (وَيَذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللهِ فِي أَيَّامٍ مَعْلُوماتٍ عَلى ما رَزَقَهُمْ مِنْ بَهِيمَةِ الْأَنْعامِ). Al-Raghib said: **Livestock** (البهيمة) refers to that which has no speech, due to the ambiguity (إبهام) in its sound; however, in common usage, it has been specified for animals other than beasts of prey and birds. The Exalted said: **‘Livestock has been made lawful for you’** (أُحِلَّتْ لَكُمْ بَهِيمَةُ الْأَنْعامِ). End quote.

He also said: **Cattle** (النعم) is specific to camels, and its plural is *an‘ām* (أنعام). It was named thus because camels were considered by them to be the greatest blessing (نعمة). However, *an‘ām* is said for camels, cows, and sheep; they are not called *an‘ām* unless camels are among them. End quote.

So, what is meant by **livestock** (بهيمة الأنعام) are the three types: camels, cows, and sheep (whether goats or ewes). The annexation is explanatory.

The sentence—meaning His saying: **and celebrate** (وَيَذْكُرُوا) etc.—is conjoined to His saying: **that they may witness** (لِيَشْهَدُوا). That is: and that they may celebrate the name of Allah during **known days** (أَيَّامٍ مَعْلُوماتٍ)—meaning during the Days of Tashriq (أيام التشريق) according to the interpretation of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام), which are the Day of Sacrifice (the tenth of Dhu al-Hijjah) and the three days following it.

The apparent meaning of His saying: **over the livestock He has provided them** (عَلى ما رَزَقَهُمْ مِنْ بَهِيمَةِ الْأَنْعامِ) is that it is connected to His saying: **celebrate** (يَذْكُرُوا). His saying: **from the livestock** (مِنْ بَهِيمَةِ الْأَنْعامِ) is an explanation of the relative pronoun (*mā*). What is meant is their mentioning the name of Allah over the livestock—the sacrificial animal—at the time of its slaughter or the like, contrary to how the polytheists used to consecrate them to their idols.

Al-Zamakhshari mentioned that His saying: **and celebrate the name of Allah** (وَيَذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللهِ) etc., is a metonymy (كناية) for slaughtering and sacrificing. This is far-fetched because there is a special emphasis in the speech on the mention of His name specifically, and the emphasis in metonymy is connected to the thing alluded to, not the metonymy itself. It appears from some that what is meant is the absolute mention of Allah's name during the days of Pilgrimage, but this is as you see [unlikely].

His saying: **So eat of them and feed the very needy** (فَكُلُوا مِنْها وَأَطْعِمُوا الْبائِسَ الْفَقِيرَ). **The very needy** (البائس) is from *bu’s* (البؤس), which is severe harm and need. That which the speech contains is a ruling that is both permissive and mandatory.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*ثُمَّ لْيَقْضُوا تَفَثَهُمْ وَلْيُوفُوا نُذُورَهُمْ وَلْيَطَّوَّفُوا بِالْبَيْتِ الْعَتِيقِ*» — **Then let them finish with their self-neglect and fulfill their vows, and go around the Ancient House.** (22:29). **Self-neglect** (التفث) is the dishevelment of the body. **Finishing with self-neglect** (قضاء التفث) is the removal of the dishevelment that occurred due to the state of consecration (الإحرام) by clipping nails, removing hair, and the like. It is a metonymy for exiting the state of consecration.

What is meant by His saying: **and fulfill their vows** (وَلْيُوفُوا نُذُورَهُمْ) is completing what became obligatory upon them through a vow (نذر) or the like. And by His saying: **and go around the Ancient House** (وَلْيَطَّوَّفُوا بِالْبَيْتِ الْعَتِيقِ) is the Circumambulation of Women (طواف النساء) according to the interpretation of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام); for exiting the state of consecration makes lawful for him everything that was prohibited by it except women, who become lawful through the Circumambulation of Women, which is the final act.

**The Ancient House** (البيت العتيق) is the Noble Kaaba. It was named thus due to its antiquity, for it is the first house built for the worship of Allah, as the Exalted said: **‘Indeed the first house to be set up for mankind is the one at Bakkah, blessed and a guidance for all nations.’** (3:96). Approximately four thousand years have passed over this House today while it is inhabited, and on the day these verses were revealed, it was more than two thousand five hundred years old.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*ذلِكَ وَمَنْ يُعَظِّمْ حُرُماتِ اللهِ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَهُ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِ*» — **That [is the command]. And whoever venerates the sacraments of Allah, that is better for him with his Lord.** (22:30) to the end of the verse. **Sacrament** (الحرمة) is that which is not permissible to violate and must be observed. **Idols** (الأوثان) is the plural of *wathan* (وثن), which is a statue. **Falsehood** (الزور) is inclining away from the truth; therefore, lying and false speech are called *zūr*.

His saying: **That** (ذلِكَ) means: the matter is that. That is, the rites of Pilgrimage that We legislated for Ibrahim (peace be upon him) and those after him are those which We have mentioned and pointed to, consisting of the state of consecration, circumambulation, prayer, and sacrifice with sincerity to Allah and avoidance of polytheism.

His saying: **And whoever venerates the sacraments of Allah, that is better for him** (وَمَنْ يُعَظِّمْ حُرُماتِ اللهِ فَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَهُ) is an exhortation to venerate the sacraments of Allah, which are the matters He has prohibited and set boundaries for, forbidding their transgression and the commission of what lies beyond them. Their veneration is refraining from overstepping toward them.

What the context provides is that this sentence is a preamble and preparation for what follows it in His saying: **‘Cattle are permitted to you, except what has been announced to you’** (وَأُحِلَّتْ لَكُمُ الْأَنْعامِ إِلَّا ما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ). For the joining of this sentence to the preceding one conveys that cattle—despite being part of what Allah has provided them and made lawful for them—contain a divine prohibition, which is indicated by the exception: **except what has been announced to you**.

What is meant by His saying: **what has been announced to you** (ما يُتْلى عَلَيْكُمْ) is the continuity of the recitation. For the prohibitions of eating were revealed in Surah al-An‘am, which is Meccan; in Surah al-Nahl, which was revealed at the end of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) time in Mecca and the beginning of his time in Medina; and in Surah al-Baqarah, which was revealed at the beginning of the Migration after six months had passed—according to what is narrated. There is no reason to make **announced** (يُتْلى) refer to the future and take it as an indication of the verse in Surah al-Ma’idah, as they [other exegetes] have done.

Although the verses containing the prohibitions of eating include several matters such as carrion, blood, the flesh of swine, and that which has been consecrated to other than Allah, the emphasis in the verse—by the testimony of the preceding and following context—is specifically on that which has been consecrated to other than Allah. For the polytheists used to seek proximity in their Pilgrimage—which was the only remaining tradition among them from the creed of Ibrahim—to the idols set up on the Kaaba, on Safa, on Marwah, and at Mina, and they would consecrate their sacrifices to them. Thus, avoiding them and avoiding the consecration by mentioning their names is the intended purpose of the verse, even though eating carrion, blood, and the flesh of swine are also among the sacraments of Allah.

This is also supported by following the speech with His saying: **‘So avoid the abomination of idols, and avoid false speech’** (فَاجْتَنِبُوا الرِّجْسَ مِنَ الْأَوْثانِ وَاجْتَنِبُوا قَوْلَ الزُّورِ). For avoiding idols and avoiding false speech, although they are part of venerating the sacraments of Allah—and therefore **‘So avoid the abomination’** is branched from what preceded it of His saying: **‘And whoever venerates the sacraments of Allah, that is better for him with his Lord’**—the specification of these two sacraments for mention among all sacraments in the context of the verses of Pilgrimage is only because they were issues encountered in the Pilgrimage at that time, and due to the polytheists' insistence on seeking proximity to idols there and consecrating sacrifices in their names.

By this, it becomes clear that His saying: **‘So avoid the abomination of idols, and avoid false speech’** (فَاجْتَنِبُوا الرِّجْسَ مِنَ الْأَوْثانِ وَاجْتَنِبُوا قَوْلَ الزُّورِ) is a general prohibition against seeking proximity to idols and false speech, mentioned for the purpose of [addressing] seeking proximity to idols in the act of Pilgrimage as was the custom of the polytheists, and against naming idols over the slaughtered sacrifices. Upon this is based the branching with the particle *fa*.

In connecting the ruling of avoidance first to **the abomination** (الرجس) and then explaining it with His saying: **of idols** (مِنَ الْأَوْثانِ), there is an indication of causality (العلية), as if it were said: "Avoid idols because they are an abomination." In connecting it to the idols themselves rather than their worship, or seeking proximity, or turning toward them, or touching them and the like—despite the fact that avoidance in reality relates to actions rather than physical entities—there is an evident hyperbole (مبالغة).

It has become clear from what preceded that *min* (مِنْ) in His saying: **of idols** (مِنَ الْأَوْثانِ) is explanatory (بيانية). Some mentioned it is for beginning a point of origin (ابتدائية), the meaning being: "Avoid the abomination arising from idols, which is their worship." Others mentioned it is partitive (تبعيضية), the meaning being: "Avoid the abomination which is some aspects of the idols, namely their worship." In both views, there is an obvious affectation and a deviation of the meaning of the speech from its rectitude.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*حُنَفاءَ لِلَّهِ غَيْرَ مُشْرِكِينَ بِهِ وَمَنْ يُعْظِمْ شَعائِرَ اللهِ فَإِنَّها مِنْ تَقْوَى الْقُلُوبِ*» — **being upright before Allah, without ascribing any partners to Him. Whoever ascribes partners to Allah is as if he has fallen from the sky and been snatched by the birds** (22:31) etc. **Upright** (الحنفاء) is the plural of *hanīf* (حنيف), which is one who inclines from the peripheries toward the very center. Their being upright before Allah is their inclining away from others—namely the gods besides Allah—toward Him; thus, it is identical in meaning with His saying **without ascribing any partners to Him**.

These two—meaning His saying: **being upright before Allah** (حُنَفاءَ لِلَّهِ) and His saying: **without ascribing any partners to Him** (غَيْرَ مُشْرِكِينَ بِهِ)—are circumstantial qualifiers (حالان) for the subject of **avoid** (فَاجْتَنِبُوا). That is: avoid seeking proximity to idols and consecrating to them while you are inclining toward Him away from all others, not associating anything with Him in your Pilgrimage. For the polytheists used to recite the ritual call in the Pilgrimage by saying: "At Your service, You have no partner, except a partner who is Yours; You possess him and what he possesses."

His saying: **Whoever ascribes partners to Allah is as if he has fallen from the sky and been snatched by the birds** (وَمَنْ يُشْرِكْ بِاللهِ فَكَأَنَّما خَرَّ مِنَ السَّماءِ فَتَخْطَفُهُ الطَّيْرُ)—meaning they take him quickly. He likened the polytheist in his polytheism and his fall thereby from the highest degrees of humanity into the abyss of misguidance, where Satan hunts him, to one who has fallen from the sky and the birds take him.

His saying: **or the wind has blown him to a remote place** (أَوْ تَهْوِي بِهِ الرِّيحُ فِي مَكانٍ سَحِيقٍ)—meaning extremely far. It is conjoined to **and been snatched by the birds**, being another simile from the perspective of distance.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*ذلِكَ وَمَنْ يُعَظِّمْ شَعائِرَ اللهِ فَإِنَّها مِنْ تَقْوَى الْقُلُوبِ*» — **That. And whoever venerates the sacraments of Allah—indeed that comes from the godwariness of hearts.** (22:32). **That** (ذلِكَ) is the predicate for an omitted subject, meaning: the matter is that which We have said. **Sacraments** (الشعائر) is the plural of *sha‘īrah* (شعيرة), which is a sign (العلامة). The sacraments of Allah are the signs that Allah the Exalted has set up for His obedience, as He said: **‘Indeed Safa and Marwah are among the sacraments of Allah’** (2:158), and He said: **‘We have appointed for you the [sacrificial] camels as among the sacraments of Allah’** (22:36).

What is meant by them here are the camels that are driven as an offering (هدياً) and are marked (تشعر)—meaning their hump is slit on the right side so it is known they are an offering—according to the interpretation of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام). This is supported by the apparent meaning of His following saying: **‘In them there are benefits for you’** etc., and His later saying: **‘We have appointed for you the [sacrificial] camels’**. It was said: what is meant by them are all the signs set up for obedience, but the context does not suit this.

His saying: **indeed that comes from the godwariness of hearts** (فَإِنَّها مِنْ تَقْوَى الْقُلُوبِ)—meaning the veneration of the divine sacraments is from godwariness (التقوى) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being*. Thus, the pronoun refers to the "veneration of the sacraments" understood from the speech. Then it is as if the first part of the possessive construction (المضاف) was omitted and the second part (المضاف إليه) was put in its place, so the pronoun was returned to it.

The annexation of godwariness to **hearts** (القلوب) is to indicate that the reality of godwariness—which is guarding against and avoiding His displeasure and refraining from His prohibitions—is a spiritual matter that returns to the hearts, which are the souls (النفوس). It is not the physical body of the actions, which are movements and stillness, for these are shared between obedience and disobedience—like physical contact in marriage versus adultery, or the taking of a soul in retaliation versus through wrongdoing, or the prayer performed for proximity versus for show, and other things. Nor is it the titles abstracted from the acts, such as "goodness" and "obedience" and the like.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*لَكُمْ فِيها مَنافِعُ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى ثُمَّ مَحِلُّها إِلَى الْبَيْتِ الْعَتِيقِ*» — **In them there are benefits for you until a specified time; then their place of sacrifice is by the Ancient House.** (22:33). **Place of sacrifice** (المحل) with a *kasrah* on the *ḥā’* is a noun of time meaning the time of the arrival of the term. The pronoun in **In them** (فِيها) refers to the sacraments. The meaning—on the assumption that what is meant by the sacraments are the sacrificial camels—is that you have in these sacraments—which are the camels—benefits such as riding their backs and drinking their milk when needed until a specified time, which is the time of their slaughter. Then their *maḥill*—meaning the time of the arrival of their term for slaughter—ends at the Ancient House or by their arrival at it. The sentence is in the sense of His saying: **‘as an offering reaching the Kaaba’** (5:95). This is according to the interpretation of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام).

As for the view that what is meant by the sacraments are the rites of Pilgrimage, it was said that what is meant by the benefits is trade until a specified time, then the place of these rites and their end is at the Ancient House because the last of the acts performed is the circumambulation of the House.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ جَعَلْنا مَنْسَكاً لِيَذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللهِ عَلى ما رَزَقَهُمْ مِنْ بَهِيمَةِ الْأَنْعامِ*» — **For every nation We have appointed a rite [of sacrifice] that they may celebrate the name of Allah over the livestock He has provided them.** (22:34) to the end of the verse. **Rite** (المنسك) is a verbal noun (مصدر ميمي), a noun of time, and a noun of place. The apparent meaning of His saying: **that they may celebrate the name of Allah** etc., is that it is a verbal noun in the sense of worship (العبادة), specifically the worship that involves slaughtering and offering a sacrifice.

The meaning is: For every nation—among the previous believing nations—We appointed a worship consisting of offering sacrifices, so that they may celebrate the name of Allah over the livestock that Allah provided them. That is: you, the community of the followers of Ibrahim, are not the first nation for whom sacrifice and offering a sacrifice were legislated; We have legislated that for those before you.

His saying: **So your God is the One God: so submit to Him** (فَإِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ فَلَهُ أَسْلِمُوا). That is: since Allah, the Glorified, is the One who legislated for you and for the nations before you this ruling, then your God and the God of those before you is One God; so submit and surrender to Him by making your action sincere for Him, and do not seek proximity in your sacrifices to anyone else. The *fa* in **So your God** (فَإِلهُكُمْ) is for branching the cause from the effect, and in His saying: **so submit to Him** (فَلَهُ أَسْلِمُوا) it is for branching the effect from the cause.

His saying: **And give good news to the humble** (وَبَشِّرِ الْمُخْبِتِينَ). In it is a hint that whoever submits to Allah in his Pilgrimage with sincerity is among the humble. He has explained them by His saying: **those whose hearts tremble when Allah is mentioned, and those who are patient in whatever befalls them, and those who maintain the prayer and spend out of what We have provided them.** (22:35). The applicability of the attributes enumerated in the verse—trembling, patience, maintaining prayer, and spending—to one who performs the Pilgrimage to the House while submitting to his Lord is well-known.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*وَالْبُدْنَ جَعَلْناها لَكُمْ مِنْ شَعائِرِ اللهِ لَكُمْ فِيها خَيْرٌ*» — **We have appointed for you the [sacrificial] camels as among the sacraments of Allah. There is good for you in them.** (22:36) to the end of the verse. **Camels** (البدن) with a *ḍammah* then a *sukūn* is the plural of *budnah* (بدنة) with two *fatḥahs*, which is the fat and large camel. The context is that they are among the sacraments by virtue of being made an offering.

His saying: **So celebrate the name of Allah over them as they stand in rows** (فَاذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللهِ عَلَيْها صَوافَّ). **In rows** (صوافَّ) is the plural of *ṣāffah* (صافة). The meaning of its being *ṣāffah* is that it stands with its forelegs and hindlegs aligned and gathered, and its forelegs tied.

His saying: **And when they have fallen on their sides, eat from them and feed the self-contained and the beggar** (فَإِذا وَجَبَتْ جُنُوبُها فَكُلُوا مِنْها وَأَطْعِمُوا الْقانِعَ وَالْمُعْتَرَّ). **Falling** (الوجوب) means dropping. It is said: "The sun *wajabat*," meaning it fell and set. **Sides** (الجنوب) is the plural of *janb* (جنب). What is meant by the falling of their sides is their dropping to the ground on their sides, which is a metonymy for their death. The command in His saying: **eat from them** (فَكُلُوا مِنْها) is for permissibility and the removal of prohibition. **The self-contained** (القانع) is the poor person who is content with what he is given, whether he asks or not. **The beggar** (المعتر) is the one who comes to you and intends you from among the poor. The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «*لَنْ يَنالَ اللهَ لُحُومُها وَلا دِماؤُها وَلكِنْ يَنالُهُ التَّقْوى مِنْكُمْ*» — **It is not their flesh or their blood that reaches Allah. Rather, it is your godwariness that reaches Him.** (22:37) to the end of the verse. This is in the position of dispelling an objection, as if a simple-minded person might imagine that Allah, the Glorified, has some benefit in these sacrifices, their flesh, and their blood. So it was answered that nothing of their flesh or blood will ever reach Allah, the Glorified, due to His being transcendent above corporeality and every need. Rather, it is godwariness that reaches Him in a spiritual sense, thereby bringing those characterized by it closer to Allah the Exalted.

Or he might imagine that since Allah, the Glorified, is transcendent above corporeality and every deficiency and need, and does not benefit from flesh or blood, then what is the meaning of sacrificing these offerings? So it was answered by affirming the matter and that it is indeed so, but this sacrifice is accompanied by a spiritual attribute for the one who seeks proximity through it. This spiritual attribute is of such a nature that it "reaches" Allah, the Glorified, in the sense that it ascends to Him the Exalted and brings its possessor close to Him—a proximity with which no veil remains between him and Him to hide Him from him.

His saying: **Thus has He disposed them for you that you may magnify Allah for His guiding you** (كَذلِكَ سَخَّرَها لَكُمْ لِتُكَبِّرُوا اللهَ عَلى ما هَداكُمْ). It appears that what is meant by **magnifying** (التكبير) is mentioning Him the Exalted with greatness and majesty. Thus, the **guidance** (الهداية) is His guiding to His obedience and servitude. The meaning is: thus has He disposed them for you so that their disposal may be a means to your being guided to His obedience and seeking proximity to Him by sacrificing them, so you mention Him with greatness and majesty for this guidance.

It was said: what is meant by magnifying is knowing Him the Exalted through greatness, and by guidance is the guidance to their disposal. The meaning would be: thus has He disposed them for you so that you may know Allah through greatness for what He guided you to of the way of their disposal.

The first of the two views is more appropriate and closer to the context. For the justification according to it is by a matter connected to the context, which is their disposal so that they may be sacrificed and proximity sought to Allah through them, so He the Exalted is mentioned with greatness for what He guided to of this worship in which is His pleasure and reward. As for the absolute disposal of them for them by guiding to the way of their disposal for them, it has no specific connection to the context.

His saying: **And give good news to the virtuous** (وَبَشِّرِ الْمُحْسِنِينَ)—meaning those who perform virtuous deeds or through virtue (الإحسان), which is spending in the way of Allah.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding the saying of the Exalted: **and whoever desires to deviate therein** (وَمَنْ يُرِدْ فِيهِ بِإِلْحادٍ) etc. He said: "This verse was revealed concerning Abdullah ibn Unays, whom the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sent with two men, one a Migrant (مهاجري) and the other from the Helpers (الأنصار). They boasted about lineages, and Abdullah ibn Unays became angry and killed the Helper. Then he apostatized from Islam and fled to Mecca. So there was revealed concerning him: **and whoever desires to deviate therein wrongfully, We shall make him taste a painful punishment**—meaning whoever takes refuge in the Sanctuary through deviation, meaning by inclining away from Islam."

**I say:** The revelation of the verse regarding what was mentioned does not suit its context, nor the return of the end to the beginning and its being a completion of its meaning, as has passed.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding the saying of the Exalted: **Indeed those who are faithless** (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا)—until His saying—**and the visitor** (وَالْبادِ). He said: "It was revealed concerning Quraysh when they barred the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from Mecca." And regarding His saying: **the seeker of retirement therein and the visitor being equal** (سَواءً الْعاكِفُ فِيهِ وَالْبادِ), he said: "The people of Mecca and those who come from other lands are equal—he is not to be prevented from entering and entering the Sanctuary."

In *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain to al-Husayn ibn Abi al-‘Ala’, he said: "Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) mentioned this verse: **the seeker of retirement therein and the visitor being equal**—and he said: 'Mecca used to have no door on any part of it. The first to hang two door-leaves on his door was Mu‘awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan. It is not proper for anyone to prevent the pilgrims from anything of the houses and their dwellings.'"

**I say:** The narrations with this meaning are many, and the detailed discussion of the matter is in jurisprudence (الفقه).

In *al-Kafi*, from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr from Mu‘awiyah, he said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **and whoever desires to deviate therein wrongfully** (وَمَنْ يُرِدْ فِيهِ بِإِلْحادٍ بِظُلْمٍ)—he said: 'Every wrongdoing is deviation (إلحاد), and striking a servant without a sin is part of that deviation.'"

In it, with its chain to Abu al-Sabbah al-Kinani, he said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **and whoever desires to deviate therein wrongfully, We shall make him taste a painful punishment**—so he said: 'Every wrong a man does to himself in Mecca—from theft, or wronging someone, or any kind of wrongdoing—I consider it deviation. For that reason, he used to be wary of residing in the Sanctuary.'"

**I say:** It was also narrated in *al-‘Ilal* from Abu al-Sabbah from him (عليه‌السلام), and in it: "For that reason, he used to forbid residing in the Sanctuary." There are other narrations with the meaning of this narration and the one before it.

In *al-Kafi* also, with its chain to al-Rabi‘ ibn Khuthaym, he said: "I witnessed Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) being circumambulated around the Kaaba in a litter—while he was severely ill. Whenever he reached the Yemenite Corner (الركن اليماني)—he would command them and they would place him on the ground. He would then bring his hand out from a small window in the litter—until he dragged it on the ground, then he would say: 'Raise me up.'

When he did that several times in every circuit, I said to him: 'May I be your ransom, O son of the Messenger of Allah, this is difficult for you.' He said: 'I heard Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, saying: **that they may witness the benefits for them** (لِيَشْهَدُوا مَنافِعَ لَهُمْ)—so I said: "Benefits of the world or benefits of the hereafter?" He said: "All [of them]."'"

In *al-Majma‘*: regarding the verse, it was said: "Benefits of the hereafter, which are pardon and forgiveness, and that is what is narrated from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام)."

**I say:** Establishing one of the two benefits does not negate the generality, as in the previous narration.

In *al-‘Uyun*, in what al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) wrote to Muhammad ibn Sinan in response to his questions on the reasons [for the rulings]: "The reason for the Pilgrimage is the arrival as a delegation to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, seeking increase—and exiting from all that one has committed—and so that he may be repentant for what has passed, starting anew for what is to come. And what it contains of the expenditure of wealth, the exhaustion of bodies, and their restriction from desires and pleasures—and seeking proximity through worship to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic—and humility and submissiveness.

And the lowliness while traveling in heat and cold—and safety and fear, being diligent in that constantly.

And what that contains for all creation of benefits, and desire and awe toward Allah the Exalted. From it is the abandonment of hardness of heart—and the callousness of the soul and the forgetting of remembrance and the cutting off of hope and expectation. And the renewal of rights and the restriction of the soul from corruption. And the benefit of those in the East of the earth and its West, and those on land and sea—from those who perform the Pilgrimage and those who do not, from the merchant, the importer, the seller, and the buyer—the earner and the poor. And the fulfillment of the needs of the people of the peripheries—and the places where it is possible for them to gather. Likewise, **that they may witness the benefits for them**."

**I say:** He also narrated in it something similar from al-Fadl ibn Shadhan from him (عليه‌السلام).

In *al-Ma‘ani*, with its chain to Abu al-Sabbah al-Kinani from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام): regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **and celebrate the name of Allah during the known days** (وَيَذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللهِ فِي أَيَّامٍ مَعْلُوماتٍ)—he said: "They are the Days of Tashriq."

**I say:** There are other narrations with this meaning from al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام). There are also those that contradict them, such as what indicates that the "known days" are the ten [days] of Dhu al-Hijjah, and what indicates that the "known" are the ten of Dhu al-Hijjah and the "numbered" are the Days of Tashriq. The verse is more in harmony with what indicates that what is meant by the "known" are the Days of Tashriq.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Abu al-Sabbah al-Kinani from al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام): regarding the saying of the Exalted: **Then let them finish with their self-neglect** (ثُمَّ لْيَقْضُوا تَفَثَهُمْ), he said: "It is shaving and what is on the skin of a human."

In *al-Faqih*, in the narration of al-Bazanti from al-Rida (عليه‌السلام), he said: "**Self-neglect** (التفث) is clipping the nails, removing dirt, and removing the state of consecration from oneself."

In *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain to Hammad al-Nab, he said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **and go around the Ancient House** (وَلْيَطَّوَّفُوا بِالْبَيْتِ الْعَتِيقِ)—he said: 'It is the Circumambulation of Women.'"

**I say:** There are other narrations from them (عليه‌السلام) with the meaning of these three narrations.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Aban from one who informed him from Abu Ja‘far (عليه‌السلام), he said: "I said: 'Why did Allah name it the Ancient House (البيت العتيق)?' He said: 'It is a free house, ancient (عتيق) from the people; no one has ever owned it.'"

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: "My father narrated to me from Safwan ibn Yahya from Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) in a narration in which he mentions the drowning of the people of Nuh, he said: 'It was only named the Ancient House because it was liberated (أعتق) from the drowning.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Bukhari narrated in his *Tarikh*, and al-Tirmidhi who graded it as fair (حسن), and Ibn Jarir, al-Tabarani, al-Hakim who graded it as authentic (صحيح), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala’il* from Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr, he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Allah only named it the Ancient House because Allah liberated it from the tyrants—so no tyrant has ever overcome it.'"

**I say:** As for this narration, history does not confirm it. The House was destroyed, then altered by Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr himself, then by al-Husayn ibn Numayr by the command of Yazid, then by al-Hajjaj by the command of Abd al-Malik, then by the Qarmatians. It is possible that his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) intention was to inform about what had passed over the House. As for the narration preceding it, it has not been established.

In it, Sufyan ibn ‘Uyaynah, al-Tabarani, al-Hakim who graded it as authentic, and al-Bayhaqi in his *Sunan* narrated from Ibn Abbas, he said: "The Hijr is part of the House because the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) circumambulated the House from behind it. Allah said: **and go around the Ancient House** (وَلْيَطَّوَّفُوا بِالْبَيْتِ الْعَتِيقِ)."

**I say:** There are narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) with this meaning.

In it, Ibn Abi Shaybah and al-Hakim who graded it as authentic narrated from Jubayr ibn Mut‘im: "That the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'O children of Abd Manaf—do not prevent anyone who circumambulates this House—and prays at whatever hour he wishes of night or day.'"

In *al-Majma‘*: **‘So avoid the abomination of idols’** (فَاجْتَنِبُوا الرِّجْسَ مِنَ الْأَوْثانِ)—"And our companions narrated that playing chess, backgammon—and all types of gambling are part of that." **‘and avoid false speech’** (وَاجْتَنِبُوا قَوْلَ الزُّورِ)—"And our companions narrated that singing (الغناء)—and all idle speech—enter into it."

In it, Ayman ibn Khuzaym narrated from the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) that he addressed us and said: "'O people, false testimony has been made equal to polytheism with Allah.' Then he recited: **‘So avoid the abomination of idols, and avoid false speech’**."

**I say:** What is in the latter part was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ahmad, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh from Ayman.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Abu al-Sabbah al-Kinani from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام): regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **In them there are benefits for you until a specified time** (لَكُمْ فِيها مَنافِعُ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى)—he said: "If he needs its back, he may ride it without being harsh with it—and if it has milk, he may milk it in a way that does not exhaust it."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from Ali, he said: "A man may ride his sacrificial camel in a reasonable manner."

**I say:** He also narrated something similar from Jabir from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding His saying: **so submit to Him: and give good news to the humble** (فَلَهُ أَسْلِمُوا وَبَشِّرِ الْمُخْبِتِينَ), he said: "The worshipers."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Abdullah ibn Sinan from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام): regarding the saying of Allah the Exalted: **So celebrate the name of Allah over them as they stand in rows** (فَاذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللهِ عَلَيْها صَوافَّ)—he said: "That is when it is lined up for slaughter, its forelegs tied between the hoof and the knee." And **falling on their sides** (وجوب جنوبها) is when it falls to the ground.

In it, with its chain to Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Abdillah from Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام): regarding the saying of Allah: **And when they have fallen on their sides** (فَإِذا وَجَبَتْ جُنُوبُها)—he said: "When it falls to the ground—**eat from them and feed the self-contained and the beggar** (فَكُلُوا مِنْها وَأَطْعِمُوا الْقانِعَ وَالْمُعْتَرَّ)—he said: 'The self-contained (القانع) is the one who is pleased with what you give him and is not displeased—nor does he frown or twist his jaw in anger. And the beggar (المعتر) is the one who passes by you so that you may feed him.'"

In *al-Ma‘ani*, from Sayf al-Tammar, he said: "Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام) said: 'Sa‘id ibn Abd al-Malik came for Pilgrimage and met my father, and said: "I have driven an offering, so what should I do?" He said: "Feed your family a third, feed the self-contained a third, and feed the poor a third." I said: "Is the poor (المسكين) the one who asks?" He said: "Yes. And the self-contained (القانع) is content with what you send to him, from a piece of meat and above. And the beggar (المعتر) presents himself to you but does not ask you."'"

**I say:** The narrations regarding the preceding meanings from the Imams are many, and what we have quoted is a portion of them.

In *Jawami‘ al-Jami‘*: regarding the saying of the Exalted: **It is not their flesh or their blood that reaches Allah** (لَنْ يَنالَ اللهَ لُحُومُها وَلا دِماؤُها)—"And it was narrated that the people of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية), when they slaughtered, used to smear the House with blood. When the Muslims performed the Pilgrimage, they wanted to do the same, so it was revealed."

**I say:** Something with this meaning was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn al-Mundhir and Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn Abbas.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **that you may magnify Allah for His guiding you** (لِتُكَبِّرُوا اللهَ عَلى ما هَداكُمْ)—he said: "The magnification (التكبير) during the Days of Tashriq in the prayers—at Mina following fifteen prayers, and in the cities following ten prayers."
